params.text:
Recognising dilemmas and conflicts

A dilemma arises when your duty of care seems to pull against a person's rights and wishes, and there is no obviously "right" answer. For example, someone may want to walk to the shops alone despite an increased risk of falling, or may refuse a meal that their care plan says they need.

These situations can feel uncomfortable, but they are a normal part of care work. What matters is that you handle them thoughtfully rather than making a snap decision on your own.

When you face a dilemma:

  • slow down and consider the person's wishes, their capacity and the real level of risk
  • avoid imposing your own view of what is best
  • look at the care plan and any agreed risk assessments
  • ask for advice — you do not have to resolve it alone

params.alt: A care worker in a supportive conversation

params.text:
Where to get support and advice

You should never feel that you must carry a difficult decision by yourself. Support is available from:

  • your line manager or supervisor, who is your first point of contact for advice
  • senior colleagues and more experienced team members
  • the person's care plan, risk assessments and other records
  • specialist professionals such as social workers, occupational therapists, GPs or advocates
  • your organisation's policies and procedures

Asking for help is a sign of good practice, not weakness. Raising a dilemma early often prevents it from turning into a crisis.

params.question: A person you support wants to go out alone even though there is a risk of falling. What is the best first step?

answers.text: Discuss the risks with the person and seek advice from your manager and the team, then review the risk assessment together

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Working with the person and the team, and reviewing the risk assessment, lets you balance their wishes with a sensible plan to reduce risk.

answers.text: Tell them it is not allowed and stop them from going out

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Simply refusing removes the person's independence and ignores their rights. Positive risk-taking looks for ways to support the choice safely.

answers.text: Let them go with no discussion, because it is their choice

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The person's choice matters, but your duty of care means the risk should be discussed and managed, not ignored.

answers.text: Make the decision yourself and keep it off the record

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Decisions like this should involve the person and the team, and must be recorded. Deciding alone and not recording it is unsafe practice.
